title: Decide whether persistent web graph should share infrastructure with app backend
description: Codex strategy task. Define the architecture boundary between the product-facing app backend and the long-lived graph of scraped web content. Evaluate whether this should live in the same Neo4j database, a separate logical database on the same cluster, or a separate service entirely.
design: Current recommendation from Codex: do not merge products at the conceptual level yet. Treat the persistent web graph as an infrastructure subsystem, not the product itself. If Neo4j is used for both, prefer a separate logical database or at minimum separate bounded schemas/namespaces and ingestion pipelines. Rationale: the global graph will evolve faster, be noisier, and have different retention/query requirements than product-facing state. Shared cluster can be fine later; shared schema/source-of-truth too early is high-risk.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The decision memo compares three options: same DB, separate DB/schema on same cluster, separate service.
2. The comparison covers schema evolution risk, blast radius, data quality isolation, query patterns, cost, privacy/tenancy, and product sequencing.
3. The chosen recommendation includes a migration path if the initial boundary changes later.
4. The decision explicitly addresses whether Noos-like graph accumulation is the product wedge or supporting infrastructure.
5. The output names the canonical source of truth for raw captures, normalized entities, and user-facing app state.
notes: Claimed by Codex. Writing backend decision memo comparing same DB vs separate logical DB vs separate service.
close_reason: Completed by Codex. Added docs/strategy/graph-persistence-decision.md comparing same DB vs separate logical DB vs separate service and naming canonical storage boundaries for raw captures, normalized web graph, and product-facing state.

title: ListHub vision: publish entire personal memory with selective public/private
description:
## Vision
ListHub should eventually become the *publishing layer* for Jacob's whole personal memory (`~/memory/` and other private knowledge stores), with fine-grained visibility control so each note/file/topic can independently be public, unlisted, shared, or private.

## Why
Right now there are two distinct pools of knowledge that don't talk to each other:
1. Private research in `~/memory/research/<area>/<topic>.md` — full PII, raw quotes, source attribution
2. Curated public lists in ListHub — sanitized, hashtagged, sharable

Today they have to be maintained as paired files by hand (see sibling brainstorm ticket on the research-bundle convention). The vision is that ListHub becomes the layer that handles this automatically:

- Point ListHub at a directory (e.g. `~/memory/research/`)
- Each markdown file becomes an item, defaulting to private
- Front-matter on each file declares visibility + sanitization rules
- A `public_view:` field can point at a sanitized derivative (or ListHub auto-generates one with PII redacted)
- Edits in either place sync both ways

## Adjacent capabilities this would enable
- **Cross-referencing** between private masters and their public faces (graph-style)
- **PII detection** at publish-time (warn if a phone number, full name, or address is in a public-tagged file)
- **Topic dashboards** showing "of my N research items in this area, X are public, Y are private"
- **Memory-to-ListHub sync** as a daemon (similar to how `bd sync` works for beads)

## Inspiration
This is conceptually adjacent to:
- Obsidian Publish (but with per-note visibility)
- Quartz / digital garden tools
- The Noos vision of layered knowledge (private daily logs → curated briefing → shared graph)

## First milestone
Spec out the file-system → ListHub sync model. Design the front-matter schema. Build a one-shot import script that walks `~/memory/research/` and creates ListHub items for any file whose front-matter declares `listhub_visibility: public` (or similar).

## Related
- Companion brainstorm ticket on the research-bundle convention (paired private/public files) — that's the manual version of this
- Existing tickets: code-6gy (visibility badges), code-umo (unlisted visibility), code-gm6 (per-user sharing)

title: Define supported web surfaces and graph persistence boundaries
description: Codex strategy ticket. Decide which webpage formats this project must support first, in the context of XFeedParadise and related IdeaFlow products, and define whether persistent web-graph storage should share infrastructure with the product-facing app backend or remain a separate subsystem.
design: Premise challenge: do not collapse 'AI browser', 'XFeedParadise', and a universal graph-web backend into one undifferentiated project. First identify the beachhead user/job, then design the storage boundary to serve that wedge rather than a speculative platform. Rumelt: diagnosis first. Moore: one beachhead. Martin: where not to play.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A written support matrix exists for the first release, naming the page types we will support, the extraction goal for each, and what is explicitly out of scope.
2. A backend decision exists for persistent graph storage: same DB, same cluster but separate DB/schema, or fully separate service.
3. The decision includes reasoning about launch wedge, sequencing, operational risk, and long-term migration path.
4. The 90-day NOT list is explicit: what web surfaces and graph ambitions are deferred.
5. Follow-up implementation tickets are created for the chosen near-term path.
close_reason: Completed by Codex. The support matrix and graph-boundary decision docs are in the repo, the 90-day NOT list is explicit, and follow-up implementation tickets were created: AIB-zrl, AIB-cdn, AIB-rtj, AIB-bfo.

title: Define raw-capture and normalized-graph ingestion boundary
description: Implement the first backend boundary for the persistent web graph. Raw page captures should be stored outside Neo4j, and normalized entities/edges should be written into a dedicated graph boundary that is separate from product-facing app state.
design: Do not let a speculative universal graph contaminate product-facing state. Start with explicit boundaries and ingestion contracts.
acceptance_criteria:
1. A capture contract exists for raw artifacts, extracted metadata, normalized entities, and graph edges.
2. Raw captures are modeled separately from normalized graph writes.
3. The implementation identifies or scaffolds the storage boundary for product-facing app state versus persistent web graph state.
4. The code/docs name the canonical source of truth for each layer.
5. The design keeps open a migration path from same-cluster separate DB to separate service.

title: Add structured extraction envelopes for v1 surfaces
description: Extend the extraction pipeline so v1 surfaces are passed to the LLM as structured artifacts rather than mostly raw body text. Focus on feed/timeline, post/thread detail, article, profile, and generic link preview.
design: The LLM should normalize structured extractions, not reconstruct the page from body.innerText. This ticket is central to the v1 scope.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Extraction payloads preserve surface-specific fields for the v1 support set.
2. Feed and thread surfaces include segmented items and relationship hints.
3. Profile surfaces include identity and stats fields.
4. Link-preview/generic destination surfaces include coarse metadata without overcommitting to full parsing.
5. The transform layer can consume structured artifacts instead of a single giant text blob.

title: ListHub: Table of contents + collapsible sections + expand-all toggle for long markdown items
description:
## Motivation Long, sectioned markdown lists on ListHub are currently hard to scan. The concrete trigger is the just-published [@jacobreal/depression-resources](https://listhub.globalbr.ai/@jacobreal/depression-resources) — a long resource list with many H2/H3 sections. A reader landing on it has no overview of the structure, no way to jump to a specific section, and no way to fold sections they're not currently interested in. The page is essentially a wall of text. This problem will get worse as ListHub becomes the publishing layer for personal memory (per AIB-5ss): research bundles, curriculum maps, resource indexes, and noos exports will all tend to be long and sectioned. The sister project `markdown-reader` (OpenMarkdownReader, the desktop app at `/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/`) already solves all three of these problems with a clean implementation we can mirror. This ticket is about porting that UX to ListHub's server-rendered Jinja2 templates. ## Current state (verified 2026-04-07) ListHub renders markdown server-side via Python's `markdown` library in `/Users/Jacob/listhub/views.py`: ```python def render_md(text): return markdown.markdown( text or '', extensions=['tables', 'fenced_code', 'toc', 'nl2br', 'sane_lists'] ) ``` Notable findings: - **The `toc` extension is already loaded** but never consumed. It auto-assigns `id="..."` attributes to every heading (so anchor links / `#section` jumps already work), but no template inserts a `[TOC]` marker or accesses `Markdown(...).toc` to actually display a table of contents. - **No collapsible sections.** The item view template (`/Users/Jacob/listhub/templates/item.html`) just dumps `{{ rendered_content|safe }}` into a `.content-body` div with zero JS post-processing. - **No expand-all / collapse-all.** N/A — there are no collapsible sections to control. - **Layout context:** `templates/base.html` already has an `app-layout` with a left sidebar (`_sidebar.html`) and a `
`. The item page has room on the right for a sticky ToC column on wide viewports. There is no JS framework — just static `static/style.css`, `static/sidebar.css`, `static/sidebar.js`. This is a good fit for a small vanilla-JS enhancement. So: **none of the three features exist on ListHub today.** Heading anchors do exist (free from the `toc` extension), which makes implementation easier. ## Desired behavior ### 1. Auto-generated table of contents - Builds from the `

`–`

` headings already present in `rendered_content`. - Indented by heading level (H2 < H3 < H4 visually nested). - Click a ToC entry → smooth-scroll to that heading. If the target heading is inside a collapsed section, auto-expand its ancestors first (mirror markdown-reader's `expandSectionAncestors`). - **Sticky** on wide viewports (≥1100px or so): pinned to the right of the content column, scrolls with the page. On narrow viewports: collapses to a single "Contents" disclosure at the top of the page (or a floating button), so it doesn't eat mobile screen real estate. - **Active-section highlight** as the user scrolls (use `IntersectionObserver`, not scroll events — see CSS-Tricks reference below). - Hidden entirely if the document has fewer than ~3 headings (don't clutter short notes). ### 2. Collapsible H2/H3 sections - Each heading gets a small chevron toggle (▸ / ▾) that expands/collapses the body of its section. - A "section" = the heading and everything until the next sibling heading of equal or lower level (standard outline semantics). - Click the chevron → toggle. Click the heading text itself → still does normal anchor-link behavior (so deep links keep working). - Default state: all expanded (the page reads top-to-bottom on first load). - Persist collapse state in `localStorage` keyed by the page slug, so a returning reader keeps their view. (Optional polish — see acceptance criteria.) - Empty sections (heading with no body) get a faded toggle and aren't interactive. ### 3. Expand-all / collapse-all - A single button-pair (or one toggle button) lives **inside the ToC panel header** ("Expand all" / "Collapse all"). - Clicking it expands or collapses every section on the page in one go. - If currently mixed/all-expanded → button shows "Collapse all". If all collapsed → "Expand all". (Or just a two-button row, simpler.) - Updates the persisted state. ## Reference implementation (markdown-reader) The markdown-reader project already has features 1 and 2 implemented in vanilla JS. File paths and key functions: - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/index.html` lines 76–80** — the ToC toggle button markup. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/index.html` ~166+** — the `#toc-panel`, `#toc-content`, `#toc-close` markup that gets appended next to the main content. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` lines 3585–3632** — `extractHeadings()`, `renderTOC()`, and `toggleTOC()`. These are ~50 lines total and almost directly copy-pastable. ToC items are rendered as: ```html ${text} ``` with click handlers that scroll to the target and call `expandSectionAncestors`. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` lines 3862–3934** — `buildCollapsibleSections()`. This is the **key reusable algorithm**: it walks the rendered markdown body, groups headings + their following content into nested `
` wrappers with a `
` inside each. Stack-based, handles nested levels correctly, ~70 lines. **This is the function to port.** - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` line 3850** — the `collapseToggle` button injected into each heading: ``. (In ListHub we'd inject this client-side after render, since render is server-side.) - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` lines 3949–3963** — `expandSectionAncestors(target)`. Walks up `.md-section` parents and removes the `collapsed` class. Needed so deep links / ToC clicks always reveal the target. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/renderer.js` lines 5481–5497** — the click delegation handler on `.collapse-toggle`. Single delegated listener on the markdown body, ~15 lines. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/styles.css` lines 1580–1614** — the CSS for `.md-section`, `.md-section-body`, `.md-section.collapsed`, the rotating chevron, and faded empty-section toggles. - **`/Users/jacobcole/code/markdown-reader/styles.css` lines 2142–2261** — `.toc-panel`, `.toc-header`, `.toc-content`, `.toc-item[data-level="N"]` indentation rules, and the `.toc-toggle-btn`. **What markdown-reader does NOT have yet:** expand-all / collapse-all. They have an open ticket for it (`markdown-reader-r12`). So this is genuinely new work for both projects, and ListHub will leapfrog markdown-reader on that one feature. ## Proposed approach **Server-side vs client-side: client-side, post-render JS enhancement.** Reasoning: 1. ListHub is server-rendered Jinja2 with no JS framework. Adding a small ~150-line vanilla JS file fits the existing style (`static/sidebar.js` is the precedent). 2. Heading IDs are already generated server-side by Python-markdown's `toc` extension, so anchors and deep links work without any backend change. 3. Building the ToC and collapsible wrappers client-side means **zero changes to `views.py` or `render_md()`** — we don't have to risk breaking the existing markdown pipeline. The HTML payload stays the same; we just decorate it after the DOM is parsed. 4. The same JS works for any future template that emits a `.content-body` div (item view, future preview, etc.). **Library choice: no library.** Reasons: - markdown-reader's implementation is ~150 lines of vanilla JS total. Porting it directly is faster than learning Tocbot's API. - A library like [Tocbot](https://tscanlin.github.io/tocbot/) (~3.6 KB) would handle the ToC half but not the collapsible-sections half, so we'd still need custom code for collapsibles. Not worth a dependency for just ToC. - Use `IntersectionObserver` for the active-state highlight (native API, well-supported, the recommended pattern per [CSS-Tricks](https://css-tricks.com/sticky-table-of-contents-with-scrolling-active-states/) and [Jonathan Lau](https://blog.jonathanlau.io/posts/create-interactive-toc-in-markdown-generated-content/)). **File layout:** ``` static/ toc.js ← new: extractHeadings, renderTOC, buildCollapsibleSections, expandSectionAncestors, expandAll, collapseAll, IntersectionObserver active-state, localStorage persistence toc.css ← new: .md-section / .md-section-body / .collapsed / .collapse-toggle / .toc-panel / .toc-item[data-level] / sticky positioning / mobile breakpoint templates/ item.html ← edit: add +

title: Research-bundle convention: paired private/public files for ListHub topics
description:
Brainstorm captured 2026-04-07 while organizing depression resources from a 48h iMessage thread.

## Goal
Have a clean way to keep PRIVATE research notes (with names, phone numbers, raw quotes, source attribution) AND publish a SANITIZED public version on ListHub, with the two clearly linked.

## Options considered
**A. Paired ListHub items, slug convention** — `<topic>` (public) + `<topic>-notes` (private). Pros: one tool. Cons: drifts; sources get duplicated as topic count grows.

**B. Personal memory for private + ListHub for public** ← CHOSEN FOR NOW
- Private master: `~/memory/research/<area>/<topic>.md` (full PII)
- Public publish: `jacobreal/<topic>` on ListHub (sanitized, hashtagged)
- Cross-link via front-matter (`public_listhub:` field) and footer ("Curated from private research — DM for sources")
- Same slug stem on both sides → grep-able
- Why: kind-of-data boundary, not just visibility boundary. Phone numbers belong in `~/memory/`, not on a server set up for note publishing.

**C. ListHub directory hierarchy** (`/topics/mental-health/depression/{public,private}`) — repurposing the directory feature for visibility tiers. Same drift problem as A.

**D. True graph in Noos** — each resource = node, each person = node, edges (`recommended_by`, `lineage`, `treats`, `located_in`). ListHub list becomes a *rendered view* of a graph query. Patricia Walden lives once and shows up wherever edges point. This is what Jacob actually described wanting ("knowledge graph with labeled edges"). Heavyweight — worth it once Jacob has done this exercise for 3+ topics and feels duplication.

## Cheap version of "labeled edges" in the meantime
Inside the private master file, use `[[wiki-links]]` to people and modalities:
- `**[[Patricia Walden]]** recommended by [[Jacob]] · lineage: [[Iyengar]] · location: [[Boston]]`
- When eventually moving to Noos, those `[[brackets]]` are 80% of the parsing work.

## Naming convention to lock in
- `~/memory/research/<area>/<topic>.md` = private master
- `jacobreal/<topic>` on ListHub = public version
- Front-matter on private file: `public_listhub: <url>`, `source_session: <claude session id>`
- Public ListHub item footer: "Curated from private research — DM for sources"

## When to revisit
After Jacob has built ≥3 research bundles and starts feeling the redundancy of rewriting cross-cutting people/modalities. At that point, evaluate promotion to Noos with a render-to-ListHub sync step.

title: Integrate an article-only parser branch
description: Add a dedicated article extraction branch behind article classification. Start with Mozilla Readability as the conservative baseline, while keeping open the option to swap to Defuddle later if richer markdown and metadata handling justify it.
design: This follows the Codex Defuddle evaluation: use an existing article parser, but do not make it the universal extractor.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Article pages use a dedicated parser instead of the generic main/body text extraction path.
2. The parser runs only for article-classified or strongly readerable pages.
3. The extraction result preserves article metadata and structured body blocks for the renderer and graph pipeline.
4. The implementation keeps non-article surfaces on their own extraction paths.

title: Define v1 support matrix for webpage formats
description: Codex strategy task. Produce a concrete support matrix for the web surfaces this product should handle in v1 and near-term v2, with XFeedParadise as the anchoring use case. The goal is to stop treating 'the web' as one format and instead name the actual shapes we are willing to support.
design: Recommended strategic framing: support the surfaces that let a user move fluidly between feed discovery and source consumption. For XFeedParadise, that means prioritize feed/timeline -> post/thread -> linked article/page. Search and product can be secondary. Do not promise universal webpage understanding.
acceptance_criteria:
1. The matrix names supported v1 formats, likely including: social feed/timeline, social post/thread, article/blog/news page, link preview target, search results page, and product page.
2. Each format includes: user job, extraction goal, primary fields, rendering target, and confidence requirements.
3. Each format is tagged as one of: ship now, later, or out of scope.
4. The matrix explicitly distinguishes XFeedParadise-critical formats from generic browser aspirations.
5. A NOT list exists for the next 90 days, likely excluding generic app shells, complex dashboards, authenticated enterprise tools, and arbitrary web apps.
notes: Claimed by Codex. Writing support matrix doc and 90-day scope boundary.
close_reason: Completed by Codex. Added docs/strategy/v1-support-matrix.md with v1 ship-now/later/out-of-scope surface definitions, XFeedParadise-critical path, extraction goals, rendering targets, and 90-day NOT list.

title: ListHub: Dashboard visibility filter buttons (backend exists, UI doesn't)
description:
## Problem
There's no way in the ListHub UI to see at a glance which of your items are public vs private. To answer 'what have I published publicly?' you have to scroll through everything.

## Current state (verified 2026-04-07)
The **backend** at views.py:352-360 already supports a visibility filter:
\`\`\`python
visibility_filter = request.args.get('visibility')
if visibility_filter:
    query += ' AND visibility = ?'
    params.append(visibility_filter)
\`\`\`
So `https://listhub.globalbr.ai/dashboard?visibility=public` works *today* — but only if you know to type it.

The **dashboard.html template** does NOT expose this. No filter buttons. (Verified: `grep -n 'visibility_filter\|visibility=' templates/dashboard.html` returns nothing.)

## Proposed UI
On the dashboard, add a filter chip row above the item list:
\`[ All ] [ Public ] [ Unlisted ] [ Shared ] [ Private ]\`
- Active chip highlighted, others muted
- Clicks set the `?visibility=` query param and reload (or AJAX)
- Chip labels also show counts: \`Public (12)\`, \`Private (47)\`, etc.

## Why this is small
- Backend: done
- Template: ~30 lines of Jinja2 + a tiny CSS block
- Counts: one extra query

## Related
- code-6gy (P2) — visibility badges on items in lists/dashboards. The badges are the per-item indicator; this filter is the per-view aggregate. They go together.
- Could be bundled into the same PR as code-6gy.

title: Fix page classification and remove article fallback for unknown pages
description: Implement the immediate extraction-scope fixes required by the v1 support matrix. The current classifier can mislabel pages as search results because of operator precedence, and unknown pages are currently pushed into the article schema by default.
design: This is the lowest-risk implementation ticket that directly follows from the strategy docs. It reduces false confidence and keeps unsupported surfaces honest.
acceptance_criteria:
1. Search classification in lib/extraction/injected-scripts.ts is corrected so ?q= alone does not force search classification.
2. Unknown pages no longer default to article schema in lib/ai/prompt-builder.ts.
3. Unsupported pages fail gracefully into preview/raw handling instead of pretending to be parsed articles.
4. Tests or at least fixture-based examples cover the corrected classification behavior.
notes: Claimed by Codex. Fixing search precedence bug and removing article fallback for unknown pages.
close_reason: Completed by Codex. Fixed search classification precedence in lib/extraction/injected-scripts.ts, removed article fallback for unknown pages in lib/ai/prompt-builder.ts, and added raw-preview fallback in lib/ai/transform.ts plus manual classification fixtures.

title: ListHub: Save/favorite lists you like
description: Add ability for users to save/bookmark/favorite lists created by others. Needs a new table (e.g. saved_item with user_id + item_id) and UI to view saved lists. Like a 'star' or 'save' button on public lists.

title: Set up pipecat-listener project on HostMyApple
ListHub: Agent self-service onboarding (signup, API key, MCP/skill)
description:
Make it trivial for AI agents to onboard themselves to ListHub without human intervention. Three parts:

1. **Agent self-registration**: API endpoint for agents to create accounts programmatically (POST /api/v1/auth/register). No CAPTCHA/email verification for agent accounts — just username + optional display name. Return credentials immediately.

2. **Agent API key provisioning**: After registration (or for existing accounts), agents should be able to create their own API keys via the API without needing a browser session. Currently key management requires @login_required (session only) — need to support Bearer token auth for key creation too.

3. **Agent discovery layer**: Make ListHub's capabilities obvious to agents:
   - llms.txt at root describing the API and how to self-onboard
   - MCP server (so Claude Code, Cursor, etc. can natively connect)
   - Claude Code skill (SKILL.md) for quick integration
   - OpenAPI spec / agents.json
   - The /api/docs page should have a machine-readable section, not just HTML

4. **Documentation**: The agent onboarding flow should be THE first thing in the API docs. Not buried — front and center. 'Here is how your agent gets started in 3 API calls.'

DISCUSS BEFORE IMPLEMENTING — this has security implications (abuse, spam accounts, rate limiting). Need to decide: open registration vs invite codes, rate limits, account tiers, etc.

title: Apply test redline edits to Advisory Services Agreement docx
description: Use redline-docx to apply tracked insert/delete edits to the specified Advisory Services Agreement document and save output.
close_reason: Created tracked-changes output docx with test insert/delete edits.

title: ListHub: Real-time collaborative editing
description: Add real-time collaborative editing for shared items (like Google Docs). Would need WebSocket/SSE layer + CRDT or OT for conflict resolution. This is a stretch goal that builds on per-user write sharing (code-xxx). Consider: markdown-based OT is simpler than rich text, could use Yjs. Low priority since the git-based workflow already handles async collaboration.

title: ListHub: Make lists publicly editable
description: Add ability for lists to be publicly editable (anyone can add/edit items). Currently items are only editable by owner. Could use a new visibility level like 'public-edit' or a separate 'editable' flag on the item/share table.

title: ListHub: Show visibility badges on items in explore/directory/profile
description: Display the visibility status (public, unlisted, shared, private) as a badge/icon on each item wherever items are listed: explore page, user profiles, dashboard, search results. Currently there's no visual indicator of an item's sharing status. Users should be able to see at a glance what's public vs shared vs unlisted. Also show who an item is shared with (e.g., '3 people') on the item detail page.

title: Noos: Design curated lists with ordering, subheadings, and anonymous posting
description:
Design questions for Noos curated lists:

1. **Anonymous posting** - Allow public posts where author is hidden but owner retains edit rights. Use case: sharing curated research without personal branding.

2. **Custom ordering** - Currently items display in reverse chronological order. Need ability to:
   - Manually reorder items
   - Pin items to top
   - Sort by custom criteria

3. **Subheadings/sections** - For curated lists, ability to add:
   - Section headers (e.g., 'Paid APIs', 'Free Tools', 'Not Recommended')
   - Commentary/notes between items
   - Rich text formatting in list descriptions

4. **Display options** - Consider:
   - Table view vs list view
   - Show/hide specific fields
   - Expandable details

Context: Came up while creating 'LinkedIn Scraping Tools' curated list. Session: 48d0e63f-464b-4e05-be87-d9b6e4d64644

title: Smart Arya→Jacob message forwarding with gate script
close_reason: Deployed gate-based arya-forward script with pattern+LLM modes. Disabled old OpenClaw cron. System cron at 1min intervals with zero-cost gate check. README at ~/bin/arya-forward/README.md. Future: migrate to OpenClaw native gate (Discussion #27700) when available.

title: Workaround/Quirk catalog for recurring OS/app issues
description: Add a first-class way to capture and search known workarounds (e.g., VoiceFlow paste/accessibility permissions require removing the permission entry and re-adding due to signature changes). These are repeat offenders that should be easy to find later.
acceptance_criteria:
- Schema supports tagging an entry as a workaround/quirk (e.g., type: workaround + tags)
- Entry can store symptom, workaround steps, affected app/OS/version, and date
- Search can filter by workaround tag or type
- Capture flow can prompt for workaround metadata when detected or on user request

title: OpenLark (main): Wiki editor markdown rendering not working correctly
description:
The wiki editor in the main openlark instance (non-colony, repo: tmad4000/openlark, branch: main) does not render markdown correctly. The tiptap v3 editor with Yjs collaboration is functional for plain text and real-time sync, but markdown formatting (headings, bold, lists, etc.) is not rendering properly in the wiki pages.

Instance: openlark (main) — HostMyApple, web :7953, API :8424
NOT the colony instance (openlark-colony, branch: colony/openlark-v1, web :9175, API :3001)

Related: DocumentEditor.tsx uses tiptap v3.20.1 with Collaboration extension. CollaborationCursor is disabled (v2/v3 incompatibility).

title: ListHub: Public git repo directory at /git/
description: Consider adding a public listing of all user git repos at listhub.globalbr.ai/git/. Currently repos are accessible via git clone but not discoverable. Questions to consider: (1) Should all public repos be listed or opt-in? (2) Privacy implications — repo names reveal usernames and content slugs. (3) Could be a nice discovery mechanism. (4) Security: make sure only public items' repos are browsable, not private ones.

title: ListHub: Keep API docs and GUI in sync for sharing/visibility features
description: CLAUDE.md already says 'When you add, modify, or remove any API endpoint, you MUST update the API docs template to match.' This ticket is a reminder: as unlisted visibility (code-umo), per-user write sharing (code-gm6), and public editing (code-6fl) are implemented, the API docs at /api/docs AND the web UI must stay in sync. Specifically: (1) API docs must document all visibility values including 'unlisted', (2) Share endpoint docs must show read vs write permission, (3) GUI share dialog must expose the same options as the API, (4) No feature should be API-only or GUI-only — both interfaces should have parity.

title: Create Python library for Word tracked changes (Spire/Aspose-like)
description: Build open-source Python library to generate redline/track-changes markup in .docx, with tests vs community edition (e.g., Spire or Aspose), sample docs, and Word open validation script if possible.
close_reason: Implemented redline-docx library, tests, sample downloads, and Word automation script.

title: Investigate why Terminal app starts on macOS startup
description: Terminal.app sometimes launches automatically on macOS startup even when it wasn't previously open. Need to investigate: 1) Check Login Items in System Settings > General > Login Items 2) Check ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ for anything launching Terminal 3) Check 'Reopen windows when logging back in' checkbox at logout 4) Check System Settings > General > Login Items & Extensions for background items 5) Check if Resume (saved state) is restoring Terminal - ~/Library/Saved Application State/com.apple.Terminal.savedState/

title: Add native macOS shell wrapper for AI OS Workbench
description: Wrap web workbench in SwiftUI/WKWebView shell, add global sidebar entry points, and add iMessage/Ideaflow integration hooks.

title: VoiceFlow should default to sleep mode on startup, not green mode
description: VoiceFlow currently starts in green (active/listening) mode. It should default to sleep mode on startup. User expects it to start dormant and be explicitly activated.

title: Investigate where terminal tab is not colored
description: Terminal tabs should be colored (likely via ANSI escape sequences or shell prompt configuration), but somewhere the coloring is not being applied. Investigation needed: 1) Identify which terminal/context has uncolored tabs 2) Check shell profile (.zshrc, .bashrc) for tab coloring configuration 3) Compare colored vs uncolored environments to find the difference 4) Apply fix to ensure consistent tab coloring across all terminal sessions. Assignee: Global Assistant

title: ListHub: Auto-generate slug from list title
description: When creating a new list/item, auto-generate the slug from the title (slugify: lowercase, replace spaces with hyphens, strip special chars). Currently slug must be manually provided. Should be the default, with option to override.

title: Generate NDA redline outputs (redline-docx vs Spire Java)
description: Apply basic edits to Sample NDA via redline-docx and Spire Java compare, producing outputs for side-by-side comparison.
close_reason: Generated Sample NDA redline outputs with redline-docx and Spire Java for comparison.

title: Phase 1 Spire semantic diff validation (Java harness)
description: Implement Java-based Spire community edition harness to generate and compare tracked-change outputs for basic text edits. Validate semantic diffs (not bitwise) as Phase 1.
close_reason: Added Spire Java harness, setup/run scripts, and Phase 1 semantic diff validation with optional whitespace normalization.

title: Spire CE compatibility tests (one-to-one revisions)
description: Install Spire.Doc.Free and add cross-check tests that compare revision semantics and accepted text against redline-docx output.
close_reason: Added Spire CE one-to-one tests and validated via docker (linux/amd64) with spire-doc-free.

title: ListHub: Per-user sharing with write permission
description: The share table already supports per-user sharing (item_id, shared_with, permission) but permission is always 'read'. Implement 'write' permission so specific users can edit shared items. This is the foundation for the 'publicly editable' feature (code-6fl) — start with named-user write access, then potentially add 'anyone with link can edit' later. UI: share dialog on item page to add users + set read/write.

title: Research edge cases and add test ideas for tracked-changes library
description: Compile a list of OOXML/Word edge cases that can break tracked-changes edits; add candidate docs and test scenarios to investigate and prioritize.
notes: Added EDGE_CASES.md checklist and tests/test_edge_cases.py (empty paragraph insert, delete across formatted runs, insert with tab/line break, header/footer preservation). Pytest not available locally to run.

title: Preserve OOXML namespace declarations when saving
description: Fix Word unreadable content warning by preserving namespace declarations referenced by mc:Ignorable in document.xml/settings.xml.
close_reason: Added namespace preservation for document.xml/settings.xml and tests; regenerated NDA redline output.

title: Investigate why three VoiceFlows load on startup
description: User reports that three VoiceFlow instances load when macOS starts up. Need to investigate: 1) Check Login Items in System Settings 2) Check ~/Library/LaunchAgents/ for VoiceFlow plist files 3) Check Dock persistent-apps for VoiceFlow 4) Identify which mechanism is triggering triple launch and fix to single instance or remove if unwanted

title: Add replace-operation preset and Spire comparison output
description: Add a preset that overwrites a selected text span in the NDA sample, generate redline-docx and Spire outputs, and compare revision semantics for replace operation.

title: ListHub: Add 'unlisted' visibility option
description: Add 'unlisted' as a 4th visibility level. Unlisted items are accessible via direct URL but don't appear in the directory, explore page, or user profile listings. Like YouTube unlisted videos. Current levels: private, shared, public. New: private < shared < unlisted < public. Unlisted items should still sync to git and be accessible via the API if you know the slug/ID.

title: Regen redline vs Spire outputs and bitwise compare
description: Regenerate redline and Spire CE outputs for Advisory Services Agreement docx and compare bitwise equality.
notes: Regenerated redline output and compared checksums; Docker engine not running so Spire CE regen blocked. Need Docker Desktop/engine to rerun Spire output.
close_reason: Regenerated redline and Spire CE outputs; bitwise comparison shows they differ (sha256 mismatch). Revisions still match one-to-one.

title: Desktop Companion Creatures (Kitsune & Nymeria) - animated desktop pets that carry messages via portal
Spire CE compare redlines on Advisory Services Agreement
description: Apply Spire CE edits to the specified Advisory Services Agreement docx and compare revision outputs to redline-docx.
close_reason: Ran Spire CE edits on Advisory Services Agreement and compared revisions one-to-one.

title: ListHub: Add llms.txt and agent discovery standards
description: Add llms.txt (https://llmstxt.org/) and other agent/LLM discovery standards to ListHub. This makes the API discoverable by AI agents without prior knowledge. Could also include: agents.json, .well-known/ai-plugin.json, OpenAPI spec, MCP server manifest. The goal is that any agent can discover ListHub's capabilities just by checking standard files at the root.
